questions.question: How does UE Cast<T> work without RTTI?
options: UHT generates StaticClass() with class flags. Cast<T> compares flags — O(1), no vtable walk.
questions.correctAnswer: UHT generates StaticClass() with class flags. Cast<T> compares flags — O(1), no vtable walk.
questions.explanation: DECLARE_CASTCLASS_FLAGS generates TCastFlags<T>. Cast<T> checks UObject::GetClass()->HasAnyCastFlag(TCastFlags<T>). Fast, no RTTI.

questions.question: What does Cast<T> return on failure?
options: nullptr — always check result. Cast<T>(Obj) returns nullptr if Obj is not a T.
questions.correctAnswer: nullptr — always check result. Cast<T>(Obj) returns nullptr if Obj is not a T.
questions.explanation: Cast<T> returns T* or nullptr. Pattern: if(auto* Actor = Cast<AActor>(Obj)) { Actor->DoThing(); }. No exception path.

questions.q: What is the UE Cast<T> return value on failure?
o: nullptr — always check return. Cast<T>(Obj) returns T* or nullptr. No exceptions in UE.
questions.a: nullptr — always check return. Cast<T>(Obj) returns T* or nullptr. No exceptions in UE.
questions.e: if(AActor* A = Cast<AActor>(Obj)) { A->DoThing(); }. Null-check pattern. No RTTI, no exception. Fast O(1) class flag comparison.

questions.q: What is the difference between Cast and CastChecked?
o: Cast: returns nullptr on failure. CastChecked: asserts/crashes on failure — use when the type MUST be correct
questions.a: Cast: returns nullptr on failure. CastChecked: asserts/crashes on failure — use when the type MUST be correct
questions.e: CastChecked = 'I know this is the right type.' Crash if wrong (better than silent nullptr bug). Use in controlled paths where failure = logic error.

questions.q: How does Cast<T> achieve O(1) without RTTI?
o: UHT generates StaticClass() with TCastFlags. Cast compares class flags — integer comparison, no vtable walk.
questions.a: UHT generates StaticClass() with TCastFlags. Cast compares class flags — integer comparison, no vtable walk.
questions.e: TCastFlags<T> = compile-time bitmask. GetClass()->HasAnyCastFlag(Flags). One integer AND. Much faster than dynamic_cast (vtable walk + type_info compare).

questions.question: What are the three network roles in UE?
questions.explanation: Authority: server, has final say. Autonomous: client that owns the pawn. Simulated: everyone else's pawn. Role determines execution path.

questions.question: What does HasAuthority() check?
options: Is this machine the network authority (server) for this actor?
questions.correctAnswer: Is this machine the network authority (server) for this actor?
questions.explanation: HasAuthority() = GetLocalRole() == ROLE_Authority. Use this to guard server-only logic: spawning, damage, state changes.

o: The client that OWNS this actor — receives client RPCs, runs input prediction. Only one client has this role per actor.
questions.a: The client that OWNS this actor — receives client RPCs, runs input prediction. Only one client has this role per actor.
questions.e: AutonomousProxy = 'this is my pawn.' Input prediction runs here. Client RPCs delivered here. Server sees this actor as ROLE_Authority.

questions.q: What does GetLocalRole() return on a dedicated server?
o: ROLE_Authority — server is always the authority for all actors
questions.a: ROLE_Authority — server is always the authority for all actors
questions.e: Dedicated server: all actors have local role = ROLE_Authority. No autonomous proxies (no local player). No simulated proxies (no rendering).

questions.q: What ROLE does a pawn have on other clients?
o: ROLE_SimulatedProxy — other clients simulate the pawn based on replicated state, no input
questions.a: ROLE_SimulatedProxy — other clients simulate the pawn based on replicated state, no input
questions.e: SimulatedProxy = 'someone else's pawn.' Receives replicated position, velocity. No input processing. Smoothly interpolates server state.

questions.question: What does UFUNCTION(Server) do?
options: Marks a function as Server RPC — called on client, executes on server. UFUNCTION(Client) = opposite. UFUNCTION(NetMulticast) = all.
questions.correctAnswer: Marks a function as Server RPC — called on client, executes on server. UFUNCTION(Client) = opposite. UFUNCTION(NetMulticast) = all.
questions.explanation: Server RPC: client→server. Client RPC: server→owning client. NetMulticast: server→all clients. Must have _Implementation suffix on definition.

o: Client calls → executes on server. Reliable = guaranteed delivery in order. Must have _Implementation suffix.
questions.a: Client calls → executes on server. Reliable = guaranteed delivery in order. Must have _Implementation suffix.
questions.e: Server RPC: declared in header with UFUNCTION(Server, Reliable). Implementation: MyFunc_Implementation(). Client calls MyFunc() → server executes _Implementation.

o: Server calls → executes on server AND all clients. Used for effects everyone should see.
questions.a: Server calls → executes on server AND all clients. Used for effects everyone should see.
questions.e: NetMulticast: server invokes, all clients execute. No _Implementation suffix (called directly). Use for explosions, announcements, global effects.

questions.q: What is the difference between Reliable and Unreliable RPCs?
o: Reliable: guaranteed delivery, ordered, can bottleneck. Unreliable: may drop, no guarantee, faster. Use Unreliable for frequent/fire-and-forget.
questions.a: Reliable: guaranteed delivery, ordered, can bottleneck. Unreliable: may drop, no guarantee, faster. Use Unreliable for frequent/fire-and-forget.
questions.e: Reliable = TCP-like. Unreliable = UDP-like. Use Reliable for critical events (damage, death). Use Unreliable for cosmetic (footsteps, muzzle flash).

questions.q: What does WithValidation add to an RPC?
o: Adds _Validate function — server checks parameters before executing. Returns false to block. Anti-cheat measure.
questions.a: Adds _Validate function — server checks parameters before executing. Returns false to block. Anti-cheat measure.
questions.e: UFUNCTION(Server, WithValidation). Server calls MyFunc_Validate(params) → if false, kicks client. Validates damage values, ability usage, movement limits.

questions.question: What does UPROPERTY(ReplicatedUsing=OnRep_Health) do?
options: When Health replicates from server to client, OnRep_Health() callback is called on client — add effects, UI updates
questions.correctAnswer: When Health replicates from server to client, OnRep_Health() callback is called on client — add effects, UI updates
questions.explanation: ReplicatedUsing enables callback on value change. Used for reactive effects: damage flash, health bar update. OnRep receives the OLD value for comparison.

o: When property replicates from server to client, OnRep_Func() is called on client — add effects, update UI
questions.a: When property replicates from server to client, OnRep_Func() is called on client — add effects, update UI
questions.e: ReplicatedUsing: client receives new value → OnRep callback fires. Used for reactive UI, damage effects, state transitions.

questions.q: What is the difference between Replicated and ReplicatedUsing?
o: Replicated: silent update (client variable changes, no callback). ReplicatedUsing: update + callback function invoked.
questions.a: Replicated: silent update (client variable changes, no callback). ReplicatedUsing: update + callback function invoked.
questions.e: Use Replicated for position/velocity (handled by movement component). Use ReplicatedUsing for health (need damage flash), state (need animation trigger).

questions.q: Does the server call OnRep when it changes a ReplicatedUsing variable?
o: No — OnRep only fires on CLIENTS when the replicated value arrives. Server must manually call effects.
questions.a: No — OnRep only fires on CLIENTS when the replicated value arrives. Server must manually call effects.
questions.e: OnRep is client-only. Server: change variable → call effect function manually OR rely on the client to trigger effects via OnRep.

o: Explicit dirty marking: MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME. Only replicates changed properties. Saves bandwidth.
questions.a: Explicit dirty marking: MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME. Only replicates changed properties. Saves bandwidth.
questions.e: PushModel: opt-in. You explicitly mark properties dirty. Without it, UE compares ALL replicated properties every tick (expensive). PushModel reduces comparison cost.

questions.question: What is O(1) and when is it achievable?
options: Constant time — independent of input size. Array access, hash table lookup (average). The ideal complexity.
questions.correctAnswer: Constant time — independent of input size. Array access, hash table lookup (average). The ideal complexity.
questions.explanation: O(1) = runtime doesn't grow with input. arr[i], map.find(key) average. The holy grail. Rare for complex operations.

questions.question: What is O(n²) and why is it dangerous?
options: Quadratic — nested loops over input. 1000 elements = 1M operations. 100K elements = 10B. Unusable at scale.
questions.correctAnswer: Quadratic — nested loops over input. 1000 elements = 1M operations. 100K elements = 10B. Unusable at scale.
questions.explanation: O(n²): bubble sort, naive collision detection. Double the input = 4x the time. Must be avoided at scale. Use hashing or spatial partitioning.

questions.q: What is O(log n) and what algorithms achieve it?
o: Logarithmic — binary search, balanced tree operations. Doubling input = 1 more step. Scales excellently.
questions.a: Logarithmic — binary search, balanced tree operations. Doubling input = 1 more step. Scales excellently.
questions.e: log₂(1M) ≈ 20. Binary search, BST find/insert, heap push/pop. Any 'divide problem size by constant each step' algorithm.

questions.q: What is O(n log n) and why is it the lower bound for comparison sorting?
o: Decision tree argument: n! possible orderings, each comparison gives 1 bit → need log₂(n!) ≈ n log n comparisons. Optimal.
It could be faster with quantum computing
questions.a: Decision tree argument: n! possible orderings, each comparison gives 1 bit → need log₂(n!) ≈ n log n comparisons. Optimal.
questions.e: Ω(n log n) = lower bound for comparison-based sorting. Merge sort, heap sort, quicksort (average) achieve this. Counting sort beats it by NOT comparing.

questions.q: What is amortized O(1) and how does vector::push_back achieve it?
o: Individual push_back may be O(n) (reallocation), but averaged over N pushes it's O(1) — reallocation happens rarely.
questions.a: Individual push_back may be O(n) (reallocation), but averaged over N pushes it's O(1) — reallocation happens rarely.
questions.e: Amortized = average over sequence. Push N elements: O(N) total = O(1) amortized per push. Reallocation copies old elements — costs O(old_size).
